CPU utilization — keep the CPU as busy as possible

O Throughput — # of processes that complete their execution per
time unit

O Arrival Time(AT)

O Burst Time(BT)

O Completion time(CT)

O Turnaround time — amount of time to execute a particular

process ( CT-AT)

O Waiting time — amount of time a process has been waiting in the
ready queue (TAT-BT)

O Response time — amount of time it takes from when a request
was submitted until the first response is produced, not output (for

time-sharing environment)

ge) Shortest-Job-First (SUF) Scheduling

O Associate with each process the length of its next CPU burst
Oo Use these lengths to schedule the process with the shortest
time
O SUF is optimal — gives minimum average waiting time for a given
set of processes
oO The difficulty is knowing the length of the next CPU request
Oo Could ask the user

gp? Priority Scheduling

O A priority number (integer) is associated with each process

O The CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority
(smallest integer = highest priority)

Oo Preemptive
oO Nonpreemptive

O SJF is priority scheduling where priority is the inverse of predicted
next CPU burst time

O Problem = Starvation — low priority processes may never execute

O Solution = Aging — as time progresses increase the priority of the

process

Operating System Concepts — 9'" Edition 1.10 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2013

7 Round Robin (RR)

O Each process gets a small unit of CPU time (time quantum q),
usually 10-100 milliseconds. After this time has elapsed, the
process is preempted and added to the end of the ready queue.

O If there are n processes in the ready queue and the time
quantum is q, then each process gets 1/n of the CPU time in
chunks of at most q time units at once. No process waits more
than (n-1)q time units.

Timer interrupts every quantum to schedule next process
Performance
Oo qlarge => FIFO

Oo qsmall => q must be large with respect to context switch,
otherwise overhead is too high

Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2013

Operating System Concepts — 9" Edition

7 Multilevel Queue

O Ready queue is partitioned into separate queues, eg:
oO foreground (interactive)
Oo background (batch)
Process permanently in a given queue
Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm:
0 foreground — RR
0 background — FCFS
O Scheduling must be done between the queues:

oO Fixed priority scheduling; (i.e., serve all from foreground then
from background). Possibility of starvation.

Oo Time slice — each queue gets a certain amount of CPU time

which it can schedule amongst its processes; i.e., 80% to
foreground in RR

0 20% to background in FCFS

implemented this way
